justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental domain model change affecting the core bounded context'). Minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient'). Data migration impact noted (2M records, BR-5, AC-5, edge cases for migration failure/resumability). External API breakage identified (BR-6, AC-6/AC-7, partner opt-in mechanism). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as a dedicated escalation ('no taxonomy of what preferences exist'). Architectural concern flagged via escalations addressing multi-destination vs multi-party ambiguity and billing model. Confidence level 52 <= 75 threshold. Additionally, Bird went beyond minimum requirements with item allocation invariants, primary recipient concept, and billing ambiguity.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — identified the 1:1 to 1:N cardinality change as a breaking domain model evolution with concrete impact assessment
BR-1 clearly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
Data migration covered in depth: BR-5, AC-5, edge cases for partial failure, idempotent/resumable migration, and serving during migration window
External API breakage thoroughly addressed with backward-compatibility strategy (BR-6, AC-6/AC-7, partner opt-in)
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as a dedicated escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence at 52 appropriately reflects the significant unresolved ambiguities
Introduced Primary Recipient concept as a backward-compatibility bridge — a creative domain modeling insight not in the original spec
Billing ambiguity identified as a separate escalation — this was not in the rubric but is a genuine business-critical gap
No explicit escalation to MJ by name for the architectural migration plan — escalations are present but don't reference MJ as the target
Edge cases are exceptionally well-considered: migration during API calls, 50+ recipients, carrier capability validation, primary recipient removal
